text: The surgeon incises the peritoneum three centimeters lateral to the colonic reflection and dissects through the retroperitoneal fat to identify the transversus abdominis muscle. The dissection proceeds in the avascular plane, extending cranially onto the diaphragm and down into the pelvis past the ASIS. The dissection continues medially onto the quadratus lumborum and psoas muscle, with clear identification and preservation of the subcostal nerve. The retroperitoneal fat is visibly herniating out of the posterior lumbar defect, with the edges of the hernia defect clearly defined.
The surgeon cuts and removes a stitch from the previous repair, revealing a hernia defect measuring three by two centimeters.
The surgeon attempts to close the posterior lumbar defect using a 1-0 V-lock suture. The suture visibly pulls through the tissue, indicating its friable nature.

A seven and a half by fifteen centimeter barred soft mesh is placed into the abdominal cavity, centered over the hernia defect with overlap in each direction. The mesh is secured using a two-oh vikril stitch and is visualized to ensure it lies completely flat in the posterior space.
The surgeon closes the peritoneum using a running two-oh V-lock suture, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated smoothly.
The final visualization of the repaired area shows the surgical site with no visible signs of complications or recurrent hernia.
